text: The surgeon divides the duodenal ileal anastomosis, removing the sliced feeding tube. The proximal section is detached, and the ileum is closed with vicryl sutures, sealing the hole in the small intestine.

The surgeon divides the ileo-ileal anastomosis after removing adhesions and closing the mesenteric defect. The proximal and distal ends of the duodenum are dissected and positioned next to each other for anastomosis, restoring continuity for normal food passage. The posterior layer is sutured away from the staple line to accommodate modifications from the feeding tube removal.

The surgeon establishes support for the anastomosis to prevent drag or tension, applying a second posterior layer using V-Loc PDS sutures to ensure no leakage of fluid or air. The sutures are locked at the end, and a marker is used to identify the anastomosis site. The surgeon closes the holes in the proximal and distal parts of the duodenum.

The surgeon constructs the posterior second layer of the anastomosis using a three-row PDS suture with a V-lock O-to-Vicryl technique. Through-and-through sutures are applied to ensure a watertight seal, avoiding corner stitches to prevent excessive invagination and pressure at the corners.

The surgeon performs seromuscular and submucosal sutures on the duodenum, ensuring the sutures form a smooth, pencil-like line. The duodenum appears thickened, likely due to weight loss, affecting its absorption and digestion capabilities. Any visible defects are closed with additional sutures to maintain integrity.

The surgeon places a stay suture at the end of the anastomosis to guide the extent of the procedure. Additional sutures are locked to ensure the running suture remains tight, with a few interrupted sutures added where necessary. The anterior layer is constructed with two layers, contrary to the usual single-layer approach, particularly for the reversal of the duodenal switch.
The surgeon secures the corners of the anastomosis to prevent leaks, locking the sutures and trimming excess material to ensure a tight closure.
